figures.caption: Table 14: Summary of mathematical symbols used throughout the paper and appendix.

Figure 7: DLSP saliency curves for four MLLM backbones.Each panel shows the per-layer DLSP composite score S(ℓ) (solid black curve, Eq. (23)) together with the three constituent Z-scored metrics: Z(KL Divergence), Z(LDR), and Z(Entropy) (dashed curves). Vertical dashed lines mark each backbone’s optimal extraction layer ℓ∗. (a) Qwen3.5 peaks at hidden state 31 (score 5.406). (b) Qwen3-VL peaks at hidden state 32 (score 5.421). (c) InternVL3 peaks at hidden state 21 (score 4.154), notably lower than the other three backbones, consistent with its weaker downstream performance. (d) LLaVA-OneVision-1.5 peaks at hidden state 28 (score 5.455). All backbones exhibit a clear peak at a deep intermediate layer, with saliency dropping at the very last layers where representations become specialised for language generation.

Figure 6: Example paired synthetic calibration data from four meta-categories. Each meta-category occupies two rows: the top row is the normal branch and the bottom row is the abnormal branch. For each row, the left image shows the generated 2×2 grid (four sequential frames F1–F4), the centre text shows the LLM-generated storyboard description for each frame, and the right image shows the corresponding counterpart grid from the same scene pair. From top to bottom: Environmental Incident, Personal Emergency, Public Misconduct, Crime. Within each pair, the scene, characters, and camera viewpoint are shared; only the depicted event differs.

Figure 1: Motivation of SphereVAD on XD-Violence. Features are extracted from intermediate layers of a frozen Qwen3.5. (a) Original features in Euclidean space concentrate within a ∼13◦ cone. (b) After ℓ2-normalisation, features remain in a small spherical cap. (c) After spherical centering, features unfold over the full hypersphere with substantially improved class separation.
Table 1: VAD performance comparison. ✓/✗ indicate zero-shot and training-free status.

Table 2: Component-wise ablation. Each row adds one module to its predecessor. “Eucl.” denotes Euclidean distance-ratio scoring; “vMF” denotes vMF log-likelihood-ratio scoring. ∆ is the improvement of vMF over Euclidean scoring at each stage. Primary metrics are underlined. UBnormal does not use SGP, so M2 and M3 share the same results.

Figure 10: Domain shift between synthetic and real feature distributions. (a) Euclidean space: perdataset arithmetic means (stars) with uniform 50%-coverage spheres; straight lines show Euclidean distances from Synthetic to each test domain. (b) Hypersphere space: per-dataset Fréchet means (stars) on the unit sphere SD−1; geodesic arcs show angular offsets of 5◦–8◦, confirming a systematic rotational bias that is absorbed by unified Fréchet mean centering (Section 3.2). Features are extracted from layer 31 of a frozen Qwen3.5;3D positions are obtained via PCA and rearranged for visual clarity (Synthetic at origin/north pole, others at 120◦ azimuthal intervals) while preserving true inter-domain distances/angles.

Table 5: Complete list of the 35 sub-categories used to diversify synthetic scene generation, grouped by meta-category. These labels are used only during data generation and are discarded before inference.

Table 6: Category taxonomies of the three evaluation benchmarks versus the synthetic sub-categories. No exact label match exists between any benchmark category and any synthetic sub-category. Semantically related pairs (e.g., UCF-Crime “RoadAccidents” vs. our “Hit and Run”) differ in granularity and definition; crucially, sub-category labels are never used during inference.
Table 9: Per-stage wall-clock time (seconds) for the geometric inference pipeline. All stages run on a single CPU core after features have been loaded into memory. Data loading time is excluded.

Table 10: Computational cost comparison among training-free VAD methods on the UCF-Crime test set. “LLM/VLM Calls” counts the number of large-model inference queries required per test video. SphereVAD’s inference pipeline requires zero such calls, operating entirely on pre-extracted features.

Figure 3: Progressive improvement of feature discriminative structure. Top: geodesic distance difference ∆ = d(x,Normal) − d(x,Abnormal). Bottom: anomaly score distributions. Columns from left to right: Raw / Spherical Centering / vMF Scoring.

Figure 11: Prototype count sensitivity. Each cell shows the primary metric achieved by SphereVAD when using KN normal prototypes (x-axis) and KA anomalous prototypes (y-axis). Performance is broadly stable across a wide region of the grid: on XD-Violence the top-quartile region (AP ≥ 0.860) spans roughly half the grid, and on UCF-Crime the AUC remains above 0.840 for all KN ,KA ≥ 7. Very small prototype counts (KN = 1 or KA = 1) incur the largest drops (∼3–5%), confirming the necessity of multi-prototype representations, while excessively large counts offer no additional benefit.

Figure 5: Two-stage synthetic calibration data generation pipeline. Stage 1: Claude Sonnet 4.5 generates structured JSON scene-pair descriptions with shared context, shared characters, and fourframe storyboards for both abnormal and normal branches. Stage 2: Gemini 2.5 Flash renders each branch as a 2×2 grid image containing four temporally ordered frames.

Figure 12: Sensitivity to αG (Holistic Enhancement Strength) and βbase (Spherical Pull Strength). Each curve is obtained by sweeping one parameter over [0, 1] while holding all others fixed.Dashed horizontal lines indicate the peak performance (XD-Violence: AP = 86.99%; UCFCrime: AUC = 86.38%; UBnormal: AUC = 76.46%). βbase is not applicable to UBnormal (SGP disabled), so only the αG curve is shown in panel (c).
Figure 4: Qualitative results of SGP. Left (a)(b): Frame-level score curves (vMF-Baseline / SGP vs. GT) for two videos. Right (c)(d): Spherical visualization before vs. after pulling.
